title: What is Passive Voice?
definition: Passive voice is a grammatical construction where the person or thing that receives the action becomes the subject of the sentence. The doer of the action (agent) can be mentioned with 'by' or omitted if unknown or unimportant.
explanation: Active: 'The chef cooks the meal.' Passive: 'The meal is cooked by the chef.'
example1: Active: 'The dog bit the man.' Passive: 'The man was bitten by the dog.'
example2: Active: 'Someone stole my wallet.' Passive: 'My wallet was stolen.'
example4: When the focus is on the action or its receiver, not the doer
usage1: Passive voice = be verb + past participle

title: Why Use Passive Voice?
definition: We use passive voice to shift focus, emphasize the receiver of an action, or when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or obvious. It's essential in academic, scientific, and formal writing.
explanation: 'The experiment was conducted under strict conditions.' (focus on experiment)
example1: 'The bank was robbed yesterday.' (unknown who robbed)
example2: 'English is spoken in many countries.' (obvious agent - people)
example4: To emphasize the action/result rather than who did it
usage1: Use passive when the receiver is more important than the doer

title: When to Use Passive Voice
definition: Use passive voice when: 1) The receiver is more important than the doer. 2) The doer is unknown. 3) The doer is obvious from context. 4) In scientific/technical writing. 5) To create variety in sentence structure. 6) To be diplomatic or avoid blame.
explanation: 'The Mona Lisa was painted by da Vinci.' (receiver important)
example1: 'My car was stolen last night.' (unknown doer)
example3: 'A mistake was made in the calculation.' (diplomatic - avoids blame)
example4: Scientific reports: 'The solution was heated to 100°C.'
usage1: Don't overuse passive - it can make writing vague or wordy

title: Passive Voice Formula - All Tenses
definition: The passive voice follows: [be verb in appropriate tense] + [past participle of main verb]
explanation: Present Simple: is/am/are + PP - 'The room is cleaned daily.'
example1: Past Simple: was/were + PP - 'The room was cleaned yesterday.'
example2: Present Continuous: is/am/are being + PP - 'The room is being cleaned now.'
example3: Past Continuous: was/were being + PP - 'The room was being cleaned when I arrived.'
usage1: The tense comes from the 'be' verb, not the past participle

title: Passive Voice - Perfect Tenses
definition: Perfect tenses in passive: [have/has/had + been] + past participle. Future perfect passive: [will have been] + past participle
explanation: Present Perfect: has/have been + PP - 'The room has been cleaned.'
example1: Past Perfect: had been + PP - 'The room had been cleaned before the party.'
example2: Future Perfect: will have been + PP - 'The room will have been cleaned by noon.'
example4: Perfect passive emphasizes completion before another time
usage1: 'Been' is required in perfect passive - don't forget it!

title: Passive Voice - Future & Modals
definition: Future simple: will be + past participle. Modal verbs: modal + be + past participle. Past modals: modal + have been + past participle
explanation: Future: 'The report will be finished tomorrow.'
example1: Modal - can: 'This problem can be solved easily.'
example2: Modal - must: 'All tickets must be purchased online.'
example3: Past modal: 'The work should have been done yesterday.'
example4: Modals + be = present/future. Modals + have been = past
usage1: 'The car needs to be washed' - infinitive passive (to be + PP)

title: Active vs Passive - Comparing Focus
definition: The key difference between active and passive voice is what the sentence emphasizes. Active emphasizes the doer (subject); passive emphasizes the receiver (direct object becomes subject). Understanding when to use each is crucial for clear communication.
example1: Passive: 'Hamlet was written by Shakespeare.' (Focus on Hamlet)
example2: Active: 'The storm destroyed the house.' (Focus on storm)
example3: Passive: 'The house was destroyed by the storm.' (Focus on house)
example4: Choose active for direct, energetic writing. Choose passive when receiver/action is more important than doer.
usage1: In news headlines, passive is common: 'Man arrested' (not 'Police arrested man')

title: The 'By' Agent in Passive Voice
definition: The 'by' phrase in passive voice introduces the doer (agent) of the action. It's included only when the agent is important, specific, or adds necessary information. Often, the agent is omitted entirely when unknown, obvious, or irrelevant.
explanation: Agent included: 'The book was written by Hemingway.' (important who)
example1: Agent omitted - unknown: 'My wallet was stolen.' (don't know who)
example2: Agent omitted - obvious: 'Criminals are arrested.' (police - obvious)
example3: Agent omitted - irrelevant: 'The bridge was built in 1920.' (builders irrelevant)
example4: Only include 'by + agent' when that information matters to your meaning
usage1: Overusing 'by' can make writing clunky. Omit when possible!

title: Passive Voice in Academic & Scientific Writing
definition: Academic and scientific writing heavily favors passive voice because it creates objectivity. The focus stays on procedures, results, and phenomena rather than the researcher's actions. This removes personal bias and emphasizes the science itself.
explanation: Passive: 'The solution was heated to 80°C.' (Not 'I heated the solution')
example1: Passive: 'It was observed that temperature increased.' (Not 'We observed')
example2: Passive: 'Data were collected over six months.' (Not 'We collected data')
example3: Passive: 'Three trials were conducted.' (Not 'We conducted trials')
example4: In methods and results sections, passive is standard practice. Use active in introductions/discussions for interpretation.
usage1: Some journals now encourage limited active voice. Check your target journal's guidelines.

title: Present Simple Passive
usage1: The sentence refers to a general truth

title: Present Simple Passive - Negative
usage1: Use 'are' because 'products' is plural

title: Past Simple Passive
sentence: The Mona Lisa ______ (paint) by Leonardo da Vinci.
usage1: Single event in the past - use 'was'

title: Past Simple Passive - Plural
sentence: The pyramids ______ (build) thousands of years ago.
usage1: 'Pyramids' is plural - use 'were'

title: Present Continuous Passive
usage1: Action happening now - present continuous passive

title: Past Continuous Passive
usage1: Action in progress at a past time

title: Present Perfect Passive
usage1: 'Already' suggests present perfect

title: Present Perfect Passive - Singular
usage1: 'Email' is singular - use 'has'

title: Past Perfect Passive
usage1: Action completed before another past action

title: Future Simple Passive
usage1: Future event

title: Future with Going To
sentence: The problem ______ (solve) by the end of the week.
usage1: Planned future action

title: Modal - Can
usage1: Expresses possibility

title: Modal - Must
usage1: Expresses obligation

title: Modal - Should
usage1: Expresses recommendation

title: Modal - Might
usage1: Expresses possibility

title: Modal Perfect Passive
sentence: The work ______ (finish) by Friday, but it wasn't.
usage1: Past obligation not fulfilled

title: Passive with Two Objects
usage1: Some verbs (give, send, tell) have two possible passive forms

title: Passive Infinitive
usage1: Infinitive passive after 'need'

title: Impersonal Passive (It)
sentence: It ______ (say) that he is very rich.
usage1: Impersonal passive with 'it'

title: Impersonal Passive (Subject)
usage1: Subject + passive reporting verb + infinitive

title: Passive with Get
sentence: I ______ (get) lost on my way here.
usage1: 'Get' can replace 'be' in informal passive

title: Complex Passive
sentence: The car ______ (repair) when the accident happened.
usage1: Past continuous passive - action interrupted

title: Present Simple Passive Quiz
definition: Active: 'The chef cooks the meal.' Which is the correct passive form?
question_text: The meal is cooked by the chef.
opt1: The meal was cooked by the chef.
opt2: The meal is being cooked by the chef.
opt3: The meal has been cooked by the chef.
correct_answer: Present simple active becomes 'is/am/are + past participle' in passive.

title: Past Simple Passive Quiz
definition: Active: 'The dog bit the man.' Which is correct?
question_text: The man is bitten by the dog.
opt1: The man was bitten by the dog.
opt2: The man was being bitten by the dog.
opt3: The man had been bitten by the dog.
correct_answer: Past simple active ('bit') becomes 'was/were + past participle' ('was bitten').

title: Present Continuous Quiz
definition: Active: 'The workers are building a house.' Choose the passive.
question_text: A house was built by the workers.
opt1: A house is built by the workers.
opt2: A house is being built by the workers.
opt3: A house has been built by the workers.
correct_answer: Present continuous ('are building') becomes 'is/am/are being + past participle' ('is being built').

title: Past Continuous Quiz
definition: Active: 'Someone was cleaning the office.' Passive?
correct_answer: Past continuous ('was cleaning') becomes 'was/were being + past participle' ('was being cleaned').

title: Present Perfect Quiz
definition: Active: 'They have finished the project.' Choose passive.
correct_answer: Present perfect ('have finished') becomes 'have/has been + past participle' ('has been finished').

title: Past Perfect Quiz
definition: Active: 'She had written the email before noon.' Passive?
question_text: The email was written before noon.
opt1: The email has been written before noon.
opt2: The email had been written before noon.
opt3: The email is written before noon.
correct_answer: Past perfect ('had written') becomes 'had been + past participle' ('had been written').

title: Future Simple Quiz
definition: Active: 'The company will launch a new product.' Passive?
opt2: A new product will be launched.
opt3: A new product has been launched.
correct_answer: Future simple ('will launch') becomes 'will be + past participle' ('will be launched').

title: Modal - Can Quiz
definition: Active: 'You can find the information online.' Passive?
opt1: The information can be found online.
opt2: The information could be found online.
opt3: The information has been found online.
correct_answer: Modal 'can' becomes 'can + be + past participle' ('can be found').

title: Modal - Must Quiz
definition: Active: 'You must complete the application.' Passive?
opt2: The application must have been completed.
correct_answer: Modal 'must' becomes 'must + be + past participle' ('must be completed').

title: Modal - Should Quiz
definition: Active: 'You should wash your hands.' Passive?
opt2: Your hands should have been washed.
correct_answer: Modal 'should' becomes 'should + be + past participle' ('should be washed').

title: Modal - Might Quiz
definition: Active: 'They might postpone the concert.' Choose passive.
opt3: The concert might have been postponed.
correct_answer: Modal 'might' becomes 'might + be + past participle' ('might be postponed').

title: Modal Past (Should have) Quiz
definition: Active: 'You should have told me the truth.' Passive?
opt1: The truth should have been told.
correct_answer: Past modal 'should have told' becomes 'should have been + past participle' ('should have been told').

title: Two Objects - Give
definition: Active: 'Someone gave her a prize.' Which passive is correct?
question_text: A prize was given to her.
correct_answer: Verbs like 'give' have two passive forms: the direct object or indirect object can become the subject.

title: Two Objects - Tell
definition: Active: 'They told us a secret.' Choose the correct passive.
question_text: A secret was told to us.
correct_answer: Both 'We were told...' and 'A secret was told...' are correct passive forms.

title: Get Passive
definition: Which sentence uses the informal 'get' passive correctly?
correct_answer: 'Get' can replace 'be' in informal English: 'I got lost' (not 'I was got lost').

title: Intransitive Verbs Quiz
definition: Which sentence CANNOT be made passive?
correct_answer: Intransitive verbs (like 'sleep') have no object, so they cannot become passive. Only transitive verbs (with an object) can be passive.

title: Active vs Passive - News Headlines
definition: In news headlines, why is 'Man Arrested' used instead of 'Police Arrested Man'?
correct_answer: Headlines use passive to emphasize the event/receiver, save space, and when the agent (police) is obvious/implied.

title: By Agent - When to Omit
definition: When should you omit the 'by' phrase in passive voice?
correct_answer: Omit 'by + agent' when unknown ('my wallet was stolen'), obvious ('criminals are arrested'), or unimportant ('the bridge was built in 1990').

title: Formal vs Informal
definition: Which sentence is more appropriate for a scientific paper?
opt3: You should heat the solution to 80°C.
correct_answer: Scientific writing traditionally uses passive voice ('The solution was heated') to maintain objectivity and focus on the procedure, not the researcher.

title: Passive Transformation - Present Simple Active to Passive
definition:
Active: Subject + verb(s/es) + object
Passive: Object + is/am/are + past participle + (by subject)
example1: Passive: 'The meal is prepared by the chef.'
usage1: The passive object becomes the new subject

title: Passive Transformation - Past Simple Active to Passive
definition:
Active: Subject + past verb + object
Passive: Object + was/were + past participle + (by subject)
example3: Passive: 'The house was destroyed by the storm.'
usage1: 'Was' for singular, 'were' for plural subjects

title: Passive Transformation - Present Continuous
definition:
Active: Subject + is/am/are + verb-ing + object
Passive: Object + is/am/are + being + past participle + (by subject)
explanation: Active: 'The workers are building a bridge.'
example1: Passive: 'A bridge is being built by the workers.'
usage1: 'Being' is essential in continuous passive forms

title: Passive Transformation - Present Perfect
definition:
Active: Subject + have/has + past participle + object
Passive: Object + have/has + been + past participle + (by subject)
example2: Active: 'She has sent all the emails.'
example3: Passive: 'All the emails have been sent.'
usage1: 'Has been' for singular, 'have been' for plural

title: Passive Transformation - With Modals
definition:
Active: Subject + modal + verb + object
Passive: Object + modal + be + past participle + (by subject)
example3: Passive: 'Uniforms must be worn by students.'
usage1: Passive: 'The room should be cleaned.'
image_url: The modal stays the same; add 'be' before the past participle

title: Passive Voice Structure Diagram
sentence: This diagram shows the transformation from active to passive voice. The object of the active sentence becomes the subject of the passive sentence. The verb changes to 'be + past participle' and the original subject becomes the agent after 'by'.

title: When to Use Passive Voice
sentence: This infographic illustrates the main situations where passive voice is preferred: (1) when the receiver is more important than the doer, (2) when the doer is unknown, (3) when the doer is obvious from context, (4) in scientific/academic writing.

title: Passive Voice Recognition - Present
definition: Listen to each sentence and identify if it's active or passive. Type 'active' or 'passive'.
question_text:
1. The chef cooks dinner every night. (active)
2. Dinner is cooked every night. (passive)
3. The students read books. (active)
4. Books are read by students. (passive)

title: Passive Voice Recognition - Past
definition: Listen to each sentence and identify if it's active or passive. Type 'active' or 'passive'.
question_text:
1. The storm destroyed the town. (active)
2. The town was destroyed by the storm. (passive)
3. She wrote a beautiful poem. (active)
4. A beautiful poem was written by her. (passive)

title: Passive Transformation Practice
definition: Listen to the active sentence, then say/write the passive form.
sentence: is made,was built,will be finished,can be seen
question_text:
Active: 'They make cheese from milk.' -> Passive: 'Cheese is made from milk.'
Active: 'The Romans built this wall.' -> Passive: 'This wall was built by the Romans.'
Active: 'The company will finish the project.' -> Passive: 'The project will be finished.'
